description: UBI support evolved gradually, then accelerated during COVID-19 as stimulus checks normalized direct cash transfers. Support grew from ~35% pre-COVID to peak ~55% in mid-2020, then settled ~48%. Tests crisis-driven Overton window shift and policy normalization.

key_events.description: Andrew Yang's 2020 presidential campaign popularizes UBI concept ('Freedom Dividend'). Idea enters mainstream discourse.
COVID-19 pandemic triggers massive unemployment. Congress passes $1,200 stimulus checks. Direct cash transfers become policy reality.
Second round of stimulus ($600) plus expanded unemployment benefits. Pew finds 55% support for regular government payments to citizens.
Inflation concerns mount. Critics link stimulus spending to rising prices. Conservative opposition to ongoing transfers hardens.
Multiple UBI pilot programs launch in US cities (Stockton, Chicago). Evidence-based discourse replaces pure ideology for some undecideds.

calibration_notes: Tests crisis-driven Overton window shift. Key patterns: pre-crisis fringe idea (~35% support), crisis creating experiential evidence (stimulus checks as de facto UBI), rapid normalization during emergency, partial regression as crisis fades and inflation counter-narrative emerges, permanent net shift of ~13pts above pre-crisis baseline. Hill-HarrisX, Pew Research polling data used. Strong partisan gap: Democrats ~70% support vs Republicans ~25%.
